evals.description: Well-structured article — most checks pass, structural_score above 70

input.content:
---
title: How to Speed Up WordPress in 2026
description: Discover 7 proven ways to speed up WordPress in 2026 — 53% of users leave slow sites. Includes caching, image optimization, and CDN setup.
coverImage: https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/example.jpg
ogImage: https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/example.jpg
---

# How to Speed Up WordPress in 2026

> **Key Takeaways**
> - 53% of mobile users abandon sites that load in over 3 seconds (Google, 2025)
> - Caching alone reduces TTFB by up to 80%
> - 3 plugins handle 90% of WordPress performance issues
> - Most optimizations take under 30 minutes

## Why WordPress Gets Slow

WordPress performance degrades as sites grow. According to a 2025 Google study, 53% of mobile users abandon sites that take over 3 seconds to load ([Google](https://thinkwithgoogle.com/example)). The main culprits are unoptimized images, render-blocking scripts, and the lack of server-side caching. [INTERNAL-LINK: wordpress-performance-guide]

[ORIGINAL DATA]

We tested 50 WordPress sites and found that 78% had no object caching configured.

## Caching: The Fastest Win

Server-side caching cuts TTFB dramatically. According to Kinsta's 2025 benchmarks, WP Rocket reduces TTFB by an average of 74% on shared hosting ([Kinsta](https://kinsta.com/example)). Install WP Rocket, enable page caching, and your site speeds up before you optimize anything else. [INTERNAL-LINK: wp-rocket-tutorial]

[CHART: bar — Page Load Time by Caching Plugin]
[CHART: line — TTFB Before and After Caching]
[CHART: donut — Distribution of WP Performance Issues]

![WordPress caching dashboard showing performance metrics](https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/cache-dashboard.jpg)
![Server response time comparison chart](https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/server-chart.jpg)
![WordPress admin performance settings panel](https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/wp-admin-panel.jpg)
![CDN distribution map illustration](https://cdn.pixabay.com/photo/cdn-map.jpg)

## Image Optimization

Images are often the largest assets on a page. A 2026 WebPageTest study of 10,000 WordPress sites found that 67% had at least one image over 500KB ([WebPageTest](https://webpagetest.org/example)). Use WebP format and lazy loading to cut image payload by 60%. [INTERNAL-LINK: image-optimization-wordpress]

## Database Optimization

Database queries slow down high-traffic sites. According to Cloudways, uncached database queries account for 35% of WordPress performance issues on shared hosting ([Cloudways](https://cloudways.com/blog/example)). Install a query monitor plugin to identify slow queries. [INTERNAL-LINK: database-optimization]

## Frequently Asked Questions

### How often should I clear my WordPress cache?
Clear your cache whenever you publish new content or update plugins. Automated cache clearing is included in all major caching plugins. [INTERNAL-LINK: cache-clearing-tutorial]

### Does caching work with WooCommerce?
Yes, but cart and checkout pages must be excluded from page caching. Configure exclusions in your caching plugin settings.

### What is TTFB in WordPress?
Time to First Byte (TTFB) measures how long the server takes to respond. Under 200ms is excellent; above 600ms indicates a server-side bottleneck.

### Is a CDN necessary for WordPress?
A CDN is highly recommended for sites with international visitors or media-heavy content. Cloudflare's free plan covers most small business needs.

Each check has an evidence field explaining the finding
faq_section.status is 'pass' (FAQ section present with 4 Q&A pairs)

evals.description: Article missing structural elements — several checks fail

input.content:
# WordPress Speed Tips

WordPress can be slow. Here are some tips.

## Use a caching plugin

Caching plugins help. There are many to choose from. You should really consider using one because it makes things faster and better and more efficient.

## Optimize images

Images should be optimized. Very large images can slow down your site incredibly. You might want to think about compressing them. Optimization is truly important for site performance. Make sure to do this step first.

## Use a CDN

A CDN can help distribute content globally. This is a good idea for many sites. Content delivery networks are useful tools that can significantly reduce load times for your visitors around the world.
